studies.title: Dzarvis — multi-agent assistant on Claude
studies.teaser: Architecture write-up for Dzarvis: a multi-agent harness with narrow specialized subagents on top of a 208-tool MCP server. In stealth, fine-tuning with a focus group of 15 companies.

sections.heading: What Dzarvis is
sections.body: A multi-agent assistant on Claude. Daniel runs the orchestrator + a swarm of narrow specialized subagents over a 208-tool MCP server. Currently in stealth, fine-tuning with 15 companies.

sections.heading: Why a 208-tool MCP server
sections.body: Each tool is a contract: name + Zod-validated args + return shape. The orchestrator picks tools by name, the subagents are scoped to a narrow tool subset. This pushes routing logic into the type system rather than into hand-rolled if/else.

sections.heading: Why narrow subagents
sections.body: A single mega-prompt with all 208 tools available to one agent collapses under context-window pressure and tool-selection noise. Narrow subagents get tighter prompts, smaller tool surfaces, and predictable failure modes.

sections.heading: Trust boundary
sections.body: All LLM-emitted values that touch persistence go through Zod parsing first. URLs hit an allowlist before fetch. The system prompt explicitly tells the model to refuse with a structured error envelope when input is unparseable.

studies.title: mcp.danielsuchan.dev — A hardened public MCP server
studies.teaser: Architecture writeup for mcp.danielsuchan.dev: a public, free-tier-safe MCP server with 16 tools, KV-based budget circuit breaker, sanitized errors, and 100% line coverage on logic.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body: A personal MCP server is a strange thing to run — neither product nor portfolio piece, but a typed surface for AI agents to read about Daniel without scraping HTML. Three constraints made it non-trivial: public + unauthenticated (anyone can call any tool), free tier (10K Workers AI Neurons/day), and agent-first audience (the reader is usually a model).

sections.heading: Architecture
sections.body: One Cloudflare Worker on Hono with the modern Streamable HTTP MCP transport. Tools are Zod-validated handlers in tools.ts. Static profile data plus generated JSON synced from the site's source-of-truth (changelog, bug-fixes, writing, case-studies, labs). Workers KV for the daily-budget counter, Workers AI bound directly to the Worker. A build-time sync script keeps site and MCP in lockstep.

sections.heading: Hardening
sections.body: Four pieces close the worst-case paths: (1) KV-backed daily budget circuit breaker — 80 calls/day caps Workers AI usage at ~8K of 10K Neurons; (2) sanitized errors at the trust boundary — internal failures to wrangler tail, public callers see a generic envelope with no trace IDs; (3) per-input length caps via Zod; (4) 139 tests at 100% line coverage on logic, 97% branch.

sections.heading: Agent-first surfaces
sections.body: get_agent_guide is the first-call tool — returns a decision tree mapping common questions to the right specialized tool. Static surfaces complement the MCP: /api/profile.json (JSON Resume v1.0.0), /llms.txt (AI-crawler convention), and /for-agents (human-readable doc page). Every claim on the site has a queryable source — the MCP is the API for those claims.

sections.heading: Hard problems
sections.body: Free-tier abuse: 80 calls/day is a soft cap, not a security boundary; the real defense is that exhaustion produces a clean 429 with no hidden cost. LLM output trust: Workers AI doesn't accept OpenAI-style json_object, only json_schema, which the public ai_extract_json can't construct from free-form input — so the fix is layered (temperature 0 + system prompt + safeParseJson with balanced-brace fallback). Site/MCP drift: the build-time sync script writes JSON into the MCP package; CI fails if regenerated JSON differs from what was committed.
